line: LADIES BENEVOLENT ASSOCIATION OF LITTLE ROCK-This society, the object of which is to feed, clothe and find employment for the poor, was organized about August 1st, 1875, with seven members, and has at present, a membership of about 300, composed of some of the first ladies of the city. Subsequently, this society established a hospital in the old barracks on Water street, rear of State House, supplied with all the necessary conveniences for caring for the sick- nurses, stewards, etc. and a local drug store with a competent prescriptionist. A chapel is connected with the hospital where service is held every Sunday afternoon, the ministers of all denominations officiating. Application for admission into the hospital, must be made to Mrs. M. E. Haynie, Center, southwest corner of Fourth. The officers of this institution are: Mrs. M. E. NATIONAL CEMETERY - Situated on the west side of Pine Bluff road, two and one-half miles southeast of State House. Established April 9, 1868. Col. Ed. M. Main, superintendent. The lot is a rectangle, 818 feet north and south, by 642 feet east and west, and contains about twelve acres, enclosed by a thick stone wall, upon the inside of which is a hedge of pyrus canttha. The Main entrance is from Pine Bluff road, about the center of grounds on east side. A drive extends around the lot, a few feet inside the wall, and other drives through the lot divide it into twelve sections. One of these, an oval in shape, lies nearly in the center of Cemetery and is known as the Officers Section. Near this section is the flag staff. The total number of interments is 5,584, each grave having a low marble shaft with number, and name also, where known. The entire surface of the grounds is covered with a green sward, and the walks and drives are lined with evergreens, flowers and shrubbery, which makes it a beautiful place. A handsome brick house, two and one-half stories high, with basement and mansard roof, stands near the main entrance. Colonel John Flynn, a soldier in the late war, formerly superintendent of this cemetery, died December 25th, 1875.

By act of Congress, approved March 3, 1875, the fees or commissions on money orders have been changed, so that "on and after the first day of July, 1875," the fees or commissions to be charged for the issue of domestic money orders will be as follows: On orders not over $15, ten cents; over $15 and under $30, fifteen cents; over $30 and under $40, twenty cents; over $40 and under $50, twenty-five cents. NEWSPAPER DEPARTMENT—NOTICE—Thomas L. Martin, clerk. "Any word or communication, whether by printing, writing, mark or sign, upon a newspaper, pamphlet, or magazine, or other printed matter, or upon the cover or wrapper thereof, other than the name of the person to whom it is sent, subjects the same to letter postage."—[Postal Laws, sec. 16.)

REGISTERED LETTER DEPARTMENT—Miss Frank E. Brooks, clerk. Office opens at 8 a.m., and closes at 5 p.m. Valuable letters or packages should be registered, the fee for the same, to all parts of the world, being 10 cents, in addition to the regular rates of letter postage, to be paid in stamps; i. e., stamps sufficient must be attached to letters before presenting for registration. The name and post office address of the sender must be endorsed on face of envelope. After a registered letter has been transmitted, it cannot be recalled by the sender. No letter can be registered on Sunday. The Post Office Department, or its revenue, is not by law liable for the loss of any registered mail matter. Registered letters will not be delivered to any person but the one to whom they are addressed, or to a person whom the Postmaster knows to be authorized to receive them.

Money Order Department.-A. S. Brayman, clerk. Office open at 8 a.m. and closes at 4 p.m. Absolute safety in sending money by mail is secured by obtaining a money order on another money order office. No fraction of cents to be introduced in an order. United States treasury notes, gold and silver coin, or national bank notes only received or paid. No single order issued for more than $50. Parties desiring to remit larger sums must obtain additional orders. No applicant, however, can obtain in one day more than three money orders payable at the same office and to the same payee. Money orders can also be obtained at this office upon postoffices in all parts of Canada, the German States, Great Britain, Ireland, Italy and Switzerland. When foreign money orders are paid in currency, the gold value will be transmitted by the postmaster in New York.

Postage. Free Delivery. Established January 1, 1880. The post office department promises to deliver by carriers only such matter as is directed to the street and number, and the only sure way to avoid having letters advertised is to follow the instructions of the department by addressing letters as stated. Deliveries are made at 7:15 a.m., 11 a.m. and 3 p.m. Collections are made from street boxes on each return trip of carriers, and also at 11:30 a.m. and 7 p.m. in the business part of the city. No delivery is made on Sunday, but carriers are on duty at the office after arrival of the cannon ball.

General Delivery. Window open daily from 7 a.m. to 5:30 p.m.; Sunday after arrival of cannon ball. All letters remaining uncalled for are advertised on Monday of each week, and if not claimed in fourteen days are forwarded to the dead letter office at Washington. When calling for such a letter, say "advertised," and give full name and date of advertisement.

Special Delivery. A special ten-cent stamp affixed to any article of mailable matter, in addition to the ordinary postage, insures its delivery by special carrier immediately upon its arrival at the office of destination, between the hours of 7 a.m. until arrival of cannon ball. But this delivery will only be made within the carrier limit at letter carrier offices and within one mile of post offices.

Money Order Department. Office open at 8 a.m., and closes at 4 p.m. Absolute safety in sending money by mail is secured by obtaining a money order on another money order office. No fraction of cents to be introduced in an order. United States treasury notes, gold and silver coin, or national bank notes, only received or paid. No single order issued for more than $100. Parties desiring to remit larger sums must obtain additional orders. No applicant, however, can obtain in one day more than three money orders payable at the same office and to the same payee. When foreign money orders are paid in currency, the gold value will be transmitted by the Postmaster in New York.

organization_name: United States Courts of the Eastern District of Arkansas
line: United States Courts of the Eastern District of Arkansas. The United States is divided into nine judicial circuits, in each of which a Circuit Court is held for each district within each circuit. The act of Congress, approved April 10, 1869, provided as follows: "The Circuit Courts in each circuit shall be held by the Justice of the Supreme Court allotted to the circuit, or by the Circuit Judge of the circuit, or by the District Judge of the district sitting alone, or by the Justice of the Supreme Court and Circuit Judge sitting together, in which case the Justice of the Supreme Court shall preside, or in the absence of either of them, by the other (who shall preside) and the District Judge." The State of Arkansas comprises two judicial districts, Eastern and Western. The courts are held at Little Rock, Batesville and Helena. The Eighth Judicial Circuit comprises the States of Arkansas, Colorado, North and South Dakota, Iowa, Kansas, Minnesota, Missouri, Nebraska, Wyoming, Indian Territory, Oklahoma, Utah and New Mexico. Justice David J. Brewer, of Kansas, has been allotted to the Eighth Judicial Circuit, and Hon. Henry C. Cald- well, of Arkansas, is Circuit Judge of said circuit. United States Circuit Court of Appeals to be held at St. Louis, Mo., for the Eighth Circuit by the Justice of the Supreme Court, and Justice Walter H. Sanborn, of St. Paul, Minn., and Amos M. Thayer, of St. Louis, Mo. The term begins first Monday in December at St. Louis, Mo., and first Monday in May at St. Paul, Minn.

The State of Arkansas comprises two Judicial districts, Eastern and Western. The Courts are held at Little Rock the first Monday in April and the third Monday in October; at Batesville the fourth Monday in May and second Monday in December; at Helena the second Monday in March and first Monday in October; at Fort Smith the second Monday in January and June; at Texarkana the second Monday in May and November.

organization_name: United States Circuit Court of Appeals
line: The Eighth Judicial Circuit comprises the States of Arkansas, Colorado, North and South Dakota, Iowa, Kansas, Minnesota, Missouri, Nebraska, Wyoming, Oklahoma, Utah and New Mexico. Justice David J Brewer of Kansas is allotted to the Eighth Judicial Circuit, and Hon Willis Vandeventer is circuit judge of said circuit. United States Circuit Court of Appeals is held in St Louis, Mo, for the Eighth Circuit. The Term begins the first Monday in December in St Louis, Mo, and the first Monday in May in St Paul, Minn,
